title: Papermaking with Abaca Fibre
subtitle: Soaking, blending, and forming sheets from Manila hemp
excerpt: Abaca produces some of the strongest, thinnest handmade sheets available to the home papermaker. Its long, lustrous fibres form papers with exceptional tear resistance and a translucent quality unlike anything achievable with cotton alone. The preparation differs from cotton linter in important ways and repays careful attention.
sourceNotes: Hunter, Dard. Old Papermaking. Chillicothe, Ohio: Mountain House Press, 1923. Chapter on fibre varieties and their properties.

glossaryTerms.definition: A species of banana plant (Musa textilis) native to the Philippines, grown for its leaf-stalk fibres. The processed fibre, also called Manila hemp, is used in papermaking, currency paper, and high-quality stationery.
Cellulose fibres significantly longer than cotton linter, typically 5 to 15 mm or more. Long fibres form a more interlocked, stronger network in the sheet and drain more slowly than short fibres.
A very thin handmade sheet in which enough light passes through to read text held behind it. Achievable with abaca due to its high tensile strength at low basis weight.

attrs.heading: Blender safety
attrs.body: Keep the blender unplugged when loading with fibre. Drain the vat before reaching in. Treat the blender the same way you would any electrical appliance near water.

content.text: What abaca is
Abaca
, sold for papermaking as pre-processed fibre in dried sheets or balls, comes from the leaf stalks of a banana relative, not the seed fluff of a cotton plant.
Abaca
fibre has a very high tensile strength and a natural lustre. When formed into paper, even thin sheets have a strength that allows them to be folded many times without tearing at the fold line.
The defining characteristic for papermakers is fibre length. Abaca is a
long-fibre

plant, with individual fibre bundles up to 12 mm long before blending. Long fibres interlock more thoroughly than short ones, which is why thin abaca sheets are stronger than thin cotton linter sheets of the same weight.
Soaking dried abaca
Dried abaca fibre must be rehydrated before blending. The dried sheets are stiff and leathery; blending them dry or with insufficient water will leave long unprocessed strands in the pulp.

Tear or cut the dried abaca into pieces of roughly 5 to 10 cm. Place in a bucket of cold water and leave for a minimum of 30 minutes. The fibres will absorb water and soften visibly. For a full overnight soak, the material will be thoroughly rehydrated and will blend more quickly with less motor stress. There is no disadvantage to soaking longer.

Abaca sold as a ball or pre-pulled fibres requires the same soaking but may rehydrate more quickly because the fibre bundles are already partially separated. Check by pulling a strand: fully rehydrated abaca feels flexible and slightly slippery, not stiff.
Blending requirements

Abaca blends differently from cotton linter. The long fibres wrap around the blender blade rather than being cut through cleanly. This means shorter blending times are better: overblending abaca reduces its fibre length advantage and produces a pulp closer to cotton linter quality.

Fill the blender two-thirds with water. Add a small amount of soaked abaca, less than for a cotton linter batch: a loose pinch rather than a palm-full. Pulse rather than running continuously: five one-second pulses, pause, five more, pause. The goal is fibre separation, not fibre cutting.

Test by pouring a small amount into a clear glass with water. The fibres should be separated and dispersed but still visibly longer than blended cotton linter would be. There should be no remaining bundles of unblended fibre. If bundles remain, pulse two or three more times and recheck.

Abaca can also be torn apart by hand rather than blended, for very rough rustic sheets where the long fibre length is intentional in the visual result. Hand-pulled abaca forms loosely and unevenly but has a distinct character that some applications suit.
Vat consistency

Abaca vat consistency should be slightly lower than cotton linter: fewer fibres per litre of water. Long fibres at high concentration will tangle on the mould in clumps rather than dispersing. A cloudier, more dilute vat produces thinner, more even sheets.

Formation aid (PEO) is particularly helpful with abaca. The slower drainage gives the long fibres time to settle flat on the mould rather than landing at random angles. Without formation aid, abaca sheets can have a distinct directionality: fibres aligned in the direction of the shake rather than randomly oriented.
Forming and couching

Pull the mould through the vat in the usual way. The shake for abaca should be more emphatic than for cotton linter: a clear figure-of-eight, not just a side-to-side rock. The long fibres need encouragement to cross-orient.

Abaca drains more slowly than cotton linter even without formation aid, because the long fibres form a tighter mesh on the mould surface that retains water. Allow more time before couching: the sheet should have lost most of its surface sheen and feel firm rather than floppy on the mould.
Translucent sheets
The main reason to use very dilute abaca is to make
translucent sheets
for lampshades, window panels, layered collage, and decorative use. To get translucency, cut the vat to about half the normal fibre level and pull with a fast, even lift.

Thin abaca sheets are fragile when wet but regain strength on drying. Restrain-dry them on a flat board rather than free-drying; a thin wet sheet will tear on the line. The dried sheet will be stiff, slightly smooth, and will transmit light clearly. Inclusions (pressed flowers, coloured fibres, leaf skeletons) can be embedded during formation by laying them on the mould surface before pulling the final layer of pulp.
